coredata.dc:title: Rapid aqueous preparation of carbon quantum dots and their application as nanozyme for the detection of hydrogen peroxide and uric acid
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Jiali
Zhang, Yining
Yan, Chunyan
Chen, Jin
Tang, Xiaomin
Xu, Peng
Qiu, Ping
coredata.dc:description: A green and rapid method was developed to synthesize a series of carbon quantum dots (Fe,N/CQDs, Fe,S/CQDs, S,N/CQDs and Fe,S,N/CQDs) through thermal decomposition using persimmon frost as a carbon source. All the synthesized CQDs have peroxidase-like activity, and the comparision of their steady-state kinetic parameters inferred that the peroxidase-like activity of Fe,S,N/CQDs is superior to those of Fe,S/CQDs and Fe,N/CQDs. Furthermore, Fe,S,N/CQDs exhibited good affinity towards 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and H2O2 compared to natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and other nanozymes. In theory, uric acid (UA) can produce H2O2 with the catalysis by uricase. Fe,S,N/CQDs could catalyze the breakdown of H2O2 to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can oxidize TMB (colorless) to oxTMB (blue). Based on the nanozyme-catalyzed oxidation of TMB, a simple and sensitive colorimetric sensor was developed for the quantitative analysis of H2O2 and UA. Low limit of detection (0.73 μM), wide linear range (1–80 μM) and good repeatability were obtained. The sensor was also demonstrated for the determination of UA in biological samples and satisfactory results were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Nanozyme
Peroxidase-like activity
Uric acid
Biologic samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of a colorimetric sensor for H2O2 and UA detection
Detection of UA in real samples
Steady-state kinetic assay and comparision of catalytic performan
Construction of colorimetric sensor and optimization of detection conditions
Colorimetric sensing detection of H2O2 and UA
Selective testing of the colorimetric sensor
Detection of UA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765015, 21808099), China and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001), China. Compliance with ethical standards. The study was approved by the ethics committee of Jiangxi Medical College, and their guidelines were followed throughout this study. The serum samples involved in our research were from gout individuals. Informed consent was obtained from all human participants.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21765015 , 21808099 ), China and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province ( 20192BCD40001 ), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) image and (b) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy image of Fe,S,N/CQDs. (c) FTIR spectrum of Fe,S,N/CQDs, Fe,S/CQDs, S,N/CQDs, Fe,N/CQDs and CQDs. (d) XPS spectra of the Fe,S,N/CQDs and high-resolution C 1s (e), S 2p (f), Fe 2p (g), N 1s (h).
(a) Michaelis-Menten curve for TMB and (c) H2O2. (b) Lineweaver-Burk reciprocal plot for TMB and (d) H2O2.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of TMB (a), OPD (b), and ABTS (c) catalyzed by Fe,S,N/CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of H2O2. (d) The absorbance values after catalyzing the oxidation of TMB with different material. (e) The Zeta potentials of different materials. (f) The changes in absorbance with the addition of different ROS scavengers.

(a) The UV–visible spectrum of H2O2 concentration ranging from 1 to 100 μM. (b) The linear relationship between absorbance and H2O2 concentration. (c) The UV–visible spectrum of UA concentration ranging from 1 to 80 μM. (d) The linear relationship between absorbance and UA concentration. The UV–Vis spectra absorbance at 652 nm without (e) and with (f) UA. (1) NaCl (2) KCl (3) CaCl2 (4) NH4Cl (5) urea (6) dopamine (7) glycine (8) glucose (9) ascorbic acid (10) cysteine (11) glutathione and (12) UA.
Steady-State Kinetics of the Fe,S,N/CQDs, other types of CQDs, HRP, and other nanomaterials.
Comparison of various methods for UA determination.
Colorimetry determination of UA in human serum and urine samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Rapid aqueous preparation of carbon quantum dots and their application as nanozyme for the detection of hydrogen peroxide and uric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Fourth Affiliated Hospital, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Analysis and Testing, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Analysis and Testing, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China (P. Qiu).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green and rapid method was developed to synthesize a series of carbon quantum dots (Fe,N/CQDs, Fe,S/CQDs, S,N/CQDs and Fe,S,N/CQDs) through thermal decomposition using persimmon frost as a carbon source. All the synthesized CQDs have peroxidase-like activity, and the comparision of their steady-state kinetic parameters inferred that the peroxidase-like activity of Fe,S,N/CQDs is superior to those of Fe,S/CQDs and Fe,N/CQDs. Furthermore, Fe,S,N/CQDs exhibited good affinity towards 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and H2O2 compared to natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and other nanozymes. In theory, uric acid (UA) can produce H2O2 with the catalysis by uricase. Fe,S,N/CQDs could catalyze the breakdown of H2O2 to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can oxidize TMB (colorless) to oxTMB (blue). Based on the nanozyme-catalyzed oxidation of TMB, a simple and sensitive colorimetric sensor was developed for the quantitative analysis of H2O2 and UA. Low limit of detection (0.73 μM), wide linear range (1–80 μM) and good repeatability were obtained. The sensor was also demonstrated for the determination of UA in biological samples and satisfactory results were obtained.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (2,6,8-trihydroxypurine, UA) is a naturally occurring chemical substance in the human body and serves as one of the byproducts of purine metabolism [1]. It is mainly synthesized in the liver and excreted by the kidneys, and can also be excreted through bile into the small intestine [2]. In humans, uric acid levels are typically maintained within a narrow range. It is generally believed that the concentration of UA in serum is 0.13–0.46 mM (2.18–7.7 mg/dL), and in urine is 1.49–4.46 mM (25–74 mg/dL) [3]. However, excessive production or insufficient elimination of uric acid can lead to its accumulation in the body, resulting in a condition called hyperuricemia [4]. A steady high level of UA can cause an excessive buildup of UA crystals in the joints, leading to the development of gout [5]. Additionally, hyperuricemia is closely associated with kidney disease, obesity, cardiovascular diseases, and diabetes [6]. According to recent epidemiological data, the prevalence of hyperuricemia and gout has been gradually increasing in recent years [7]. Therefore, a rapid, accurate, and stable method for detecting UA is of great significance for the diagnosis and monitoring of diseases caused by hyperuricemia.

Thus far, many methods have been established to measure the levels of uric acid in plasma and urine, including electrochemical methods [8], chromatography [9], mass spectrometry [10], fluorescence [11], colorimetry [12], and so on. Among them, the colorimetric method, as a simple, fast, visually evident, low-cost, and widely applicable technique, has significant advantages in the detection of UA [13]. The colorimetric method is conducted to rapidly get the UA content not only by measuring the change in absorbance at specific wavelengths, but also by monitoring significant the change in color.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon materials at the nanometer scale, typically with a diameter smaller than 10 nm [14]. They have excellent optoelectronic propertie [15]. CQDs often manifest as luminescent particles, with their spectral characteristics depending on their size and surface functional groups. This makes them have broad potential applications in fields such as biomedical imaging, sensor technology, and optoelectronics [16]. Interestingly, certain types of CQDs possess peroxidase-like (POD) activity [17], which is an important characteristic that has attracted the interest of researchers in the biological and chemical fields. This activity enables CQDs to mimic natural peroxidases [18], such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP), and participate in enzyme-catalyzed reactions. Thus, we can refer to these CQDs with POD activity as nanozymes. Currently, nanozymes mainly include four types: peroxidase, oxidase, catalase, and superoxide dismutase [19]. Specifically, the POD-like activity mainly manifests in their oxidation ability towards substrates, that is, CQDs can catalyze the oxidation reaction of substrates (such as H2O2) to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS). As is well known, ROS includes superoxide radicals (O2 •−), singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), etc., all of which exhibit strong oxidative properties [19]. By regulating the generation and clearance of ROS through nanoenzymes, it is of great potential importance in applications such as biosensors, photocatalysis, and environmental monitoring [20].

Meanwhile, in order to modify their properties or enhance their potential applications, carbon quantum dots can be tuned through doping with other elements to introduce new features and expand their application fields [21]. In terms of sensor platform construction, quantum dots are prone to charge migration due to their large surface area [22]. The high surface area also leads to a higher reactivity of quantum dots with other substances, reducing their stability [23]. In addition, the catalytic activity of quantum dots may not achieve the desired effect due to structural and elemental limitations [24]. However, doping with different elements can modulate the properties of quantum dots. Nowadays, it is common to optimize the optoelectronic characteristics of quantum dots, improve their stability and efficiency, and expand their application areas through doping with elements such as Zn, Fe, Mn, S, N, P, etc [25–29]. For example, the doping of heteroatoms such as N, S, and P increases the electron density of CQDs, greatly enhancing their fluorescence performance and quantum yield [30–32]. Performance-optimized CQDs exhibit diverse functionality and exceptional stability [33]. Moreover, CQDs with enzyme-like activity have garnered further attention. Especially in the medical field, CQDs serve as nanozymes with excellent catalytic performance, certain antimicrobial activity, and low cytotoxicity, leading people to often utilize them as biosensors, antimicrobial agents, and therapeutic agents [19,34]. Many researchers are also striving to enhance the biocompatibility of nanozymes and reduce their cytotoxicity, thereby achieving a wider application of nanozymes in the biomedical industry [32,35,36].

The research group synthesized Fe,S,N/CQDs materials in the preliminary stage and applied them for pesticide detection based on their fluorescence intensity [37]. In follow-up experiments, we discovered that the material also exhibits excellent POD-like activity. The focus of the current study is to devise a Fe,S,N/CQDs-based colorimetric sensor for the selective and sensitive detection of low concentrations of UA. This work utilized persimmon frost as a carbon source and successfully synthesized a serial of CQDs (Fe,N/CQDs, Fe,S/CQDs, S,N/CQDs and Fe,S,N/CQDs) using a thermal decomposition method. The experiments demonstrate that Fe,S,N/CQDs exhibit excellent POD-like activity by comparison of the other CQDs. They can oxidize colorless TMB to blue oxTMB in the presence of H2O2. By using a UV–visible spectrophotometer to measure the absorbance of the reaction solution at 652 nm, it is observed that the absorbance value increases with the increasing concentration of H2O2. Based on this, we propose a method for detecting H2O2 using Fe,S,N/CQDs and TMB. UA specifically binds to uricase, resulting in the generation of H2O2 [11]. By detecting H2O2, we can measure the concentration of UA. Therefore, our colorimetric sensor can also be used for the detection of UA. In order to demonstrate the feasibility and practicality of this method, we successfully carried out accurate detection of UA in actual samples using this approach. This indicates that our method holds significant potential for practical applications.

(NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O, NaOH, Fe(Ac)2, heptahydrate ferrous sulfate (FeSO4·7H2O), (NH4)2SO4, 3, 3′, 5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), 2,2′-azinobis (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), and uricase were all purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai). Persimmon frost was scraped off from the persimmons sold in the market. Uric acid (UA), glucose, dopamine, glutathione, and urea were provided by Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). An 8 mM stock solution of UA was prepared by dissolving a certain amount of UA in Britton-Robison (B-R) buffer solution (pH 8.5).

The UV-2450 Shimadzu Vis-spectrometer (Japan) was used to record the absorbance of the solutions in this experiment. Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha electron spectrometer (Thermo, USA) to perform elemental composition analysis on Fe,S,N/CQDs, and investigated the oxidation states of the elements present in the material. The infrared spectrum was detected and analyzed using a Nicolet Impact 410 FT-IR spectrometer. The morphology of Fe,S,N/CQDs was detected using a TF 20 transmission electron microscope (TEM; FEI Tecnai, USA).

We used persimmon frost as the carbon source and adopted pyrolysis to synthesize Fe,S,N/CQDs (Fig. S1) [37]. 1.5 g of persimmon frost and 0.15 g of (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O were mixed evenly in a 100 mL round-bottom flask. The mixture was placed in a heating jacket and heated with stirring until the solid melted. The color in the mixture was observed from colorless to pale yellow during heating, ultimately turning brown. Then 50.0 mL of 10 mM NaOH solution was transferred to the above-mentioned solution, shake well, and continued to mix on a magnetic stirrer for approximately 5 min. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Filter the suspension, and the resulting solid obtained after freeze-drying was Fe,S,N/CQDs.

The synthesis process of Fe,N/CQDs, Fe,S/CQDs and S,N/CQDs is similar to that of Fe,S,N/CQDs. However, (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O is replaced with Fe(Ac)2 and CH3COONH4, FeSO4, and (NH4)2SO4, respectively. CQDs are synthesized similarly without the addition of (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O.

We separately investigated the POD-like activity of CQDs in H2O2-TMB, H2O2-OPD, and H2O2-ABTS reactions. 100 μL of H2O2 (0.5 mM, 1 mM, 2 mM), 100 μL of Fe,S,N/CQDs (0.4 mg/mL) and 100 μL of chromogenic substrate (18 mM) was transferred sequentially into 700 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 M, pH = 4). After incubating at 50 °C for 40 min, the corresponding absorption spectra were recorded using a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a colorimetric sensor for H2O2 and UA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Various concentration of H2O2 combined with 100 μL of Fe,S,N/CQDs (0.4 mg/mL) and 100 μL of TMB (18 mM) mixed sequentially into 700 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 M, pH = 4). The mixture was incubate at 50℃ for 40 min and the absorbance at 652 nm was recorded using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. When switching to assay UA, different concentration UA solution together with 50 µL of uricase (120 µg/mL) were transferred to 140 µL of B-R buffer (pH = 8.5), and incubated at 35℃ in a water bath for 15 min. Afterwards, 600 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 M, pH = 4),100 μL of Fe,S,N/CQDs (0.4 mg/mL) and 100 μL of TMB (18 mM) were added to the above mixture. The mixture was continued to incubate at 50 °C for 40 min, and the absorbance values at 652 nm were recorded using a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

Considering the presence of various interfering substances in real samples, the impact of common interfering substances in actual samples on the sensitivity was investigated. The interfering substances involved a range of inorganic ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, NH4 +), urea, glycine, glucose, ascorbic acid, cysteine, glutathione, and hydrochloride dopamine. We added interfering substances in volumes equivalent to that of uric acid to the reaction system and assessed the impact of these interfering substances on the sensor by monitoring changes in absorbance. In addition, we also introduced a certain amount of interfering substances into the reaction system without UA, which allows us to further investigate the impact of these interfering substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of UA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The serum samples were provided by the Fourth Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University, China. We evaluated the accuracy and practicality of the colorimetric sensor by comparing the detection results with those obtained from a biochemical analyzer. After centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 15 min, the serum samples were diluted 8 times with PBS (pH = 7.4). Before conducting serum sample detection, centrifuge at 4000 rpm for 3 min. Then, use uricase, 4-aminoantipyrine, and 3-diphenylamine disodium salt to co-enrich the supernatant. After the above processing steps, colorimetric detection of uric acid in the serum samples was performed.

In addition, we also collected urine samples from two volunteers. After filtration and centrifugation at 10000 rpm, the urine samples were diluted 50 times with PBS (pH = 7.4) to obtain the supernatant. After the aforementioned processing steps, colorimetric detection of UA in the urine samples was performed.

We first characterized the morphology and size of Fe,S,N/CQDs using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a–b, Fe,S,N/CQDs are spherical in shape and well dispersed in water and it was further found that the size of Fe,S,N/CQDs is concentrated between 2.5–6.5 nm (Fig S2).

Next, we further analyzed the elemental composition of Fe,S,N/CQDs using XPS spectroscopy. From the XPS full spectrum (Fig. 1d), it can be seen that Fe,S,N/CQDs are mainly composed of six elements: C (50.87 %), O (43.33 %), N (3.03 %), S (1.03 %), Fe (1.38 %), and Na (0.35 %). As shown in Fig. 1e, the C 1s spectrum displays three peaks corresponding to C-C (284.5 eV), C-N, C-S, C-O (285.9 eV), and C = O (287.4 eV). The high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 1f) can be deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 167.9 eV and 168.7 eV, respectively, which indicates that S element is loaded on the material surface in the form of SO4 2-. In the high-resolution Fe 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 1g), four peaks can be observed after deconvolution. The peaks at 710.5 eV and 724.0 eV correspond to the Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 peaks, respectively. The peaks at 713.0 eV and 728.0 eV are their satellite peaks. This confirms the presence of Fe-O and further demonstrates the successful doping of Fe. As shown in Fig. 1h, two peaks at 399.8 eV and 401.7 eV are appeared, corresponding to pyridinic N and graphitic N. It also adds to the proof that S, N, and Fe elements are all involved in Fe,S,N/CQDs.

After verifying the catalase-like activity of the material in our experiment, we found that Fe,S/CQDs and Fe,N/CQDs also have strong catalase-like activity. For the convenience of comparing them with Fe,S,N/CQDs, the elemental composition of those materials was also analyzed. As shown in the XPS full spectrum (Fig. S3a) of Fe,S/CQDs, they are composed of five elements: C, S, O, Fe, and Na. It is worth noting that in the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S3b), a significant weakening of the peak at 285.9 eV was observed, which may be due to the absence of C-N bonds. The high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. S3c) shows peaks formed by the deconvolution of sulfate and sulfite. The peak formed in the high-resolution Fe 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. S3d) also shows no significant difference from that of Fe,S,N/CQDs. The XPS full spectrum of Fe,N/CQDs (Fig. S4a) indicates that Fe,N/CQDs are composed of five elements: C, O, Fe, N, and Na. The high-resolution C 1s, Fe 2p, and N 1s XPS spectra (Fig. S4b–d) further demonstrate the presence of C-N and Fe-O bonds in Fe,N/CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic assay and comparision of catalytic performan
ce:section.ce:para:
We used steady-state kinetics to evaluate the catalytic performance of CQDs through comparative analysis. Fig. 2 a and c show the Michaelis-Menten curves of initial velocity with different concentrations of TMB and H2O2 as substrates, respectively. And thereby the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot can be obtained (Fig. 2b and d).
1 v
=
K m
v
max
1
S
+
1
K m
In this case, [S] can be approximated as the substrate concentration. Km represents the Michaelis-Menten constant, and V max is the maximum reaction rate. The Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot can be used to calculate the values of Km and Vmax .

Table 1 lists the Km and Vmax values for natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP), Fe,S,N/CQDs, Fe, N/CQDs, Fe,S/CQDs, and other catalysts. A smaller Km value indicates a higher affinity between the catalyst and the substrate. When TMB is used as the substrate, the corresponding Km for Fe,S,N/CQDs is 0.146 mM, and the Vmax is 8.05 × 10−6 M/min. When H2O2 is used as the substrate, the Km for Fe,S,N/CQDs is 0.355 mM, and the Vmax is 15.7 × 10−6 M/min. In summary, regardless of whether TMB or H2O2 is used as the substrate, the Km values of Fe,S,N/CQDs are lower than those of HRP and other nanozymes. At the same time, its Vmax value is higher than that of other nanozymes. Therefore, we can conclude that Fe,S,N/CQDs exhibit strong affinity for both TMB and H2O2, and have good POD-like activity.

In order to better highlight the catalytic activity of Fe,S,N/CQDs superior to other catalysts in the CQDs series, we compared the Km and Vmax values of Fe,S,N/CQDs with Fe, N/CQDs and Fe,S/CQDs. As shown in Figs. S5 and S6, when TMB is used as the substrate, the corresponding Km and Vmax values for Fe,S/CQDs are 0.306 mM and 1.08 × 10−6 M/min, respectively. The corresponding Km and Vmax values for Fe,N/CQDs are 0.477 mM and 3.65 × 10−6 M/min, respectively. When H2O2 is used as the substrate, the Km and Vmax values for Fe,S/CQDs are 0.525 mM and 5.47 × 10−6 M/min, respectively. The corresponding Km and Vmax values for Fe,N/CQDs are 0.374 mM and 10.9 × 10−6 M/min, respectively. The results of steady-state kinetics also confirmed that the POD-like activity of Fe,S,N/CQDs is superior to that of Fe,S/CQDs and Fe,N/CQDs.

The POD-like activity of Fe,S,N/CQDs was studied through the colorimetric reactions of H2O2 and colorimetric substrate. According to Fig. 3 a–c, it can be seen that the absorbance value at the maximum absorption wavelength significantly increases in the reaction system when H2O2, Fe,S,N/CQDs, and colorimetric substrate (TMB, OPD and ABTS) are present simultaneously, compared to the case where H2O2 or Fe,S,N/CQDs are not added. The absorbance value also increases with increasing H2O2 concentration. The reaction system can be visibly observed to change from colorless to a significantly distinct color (Fig. S7).

To explore the promoting effect of S and Fe, N elements on the catalytic activity of CQDs, we compared the catalytic effect of five materials on substrate TMB: Fe,S,N/CQDs, Fe,N/CQDs, Fe,S /CQDs, S,N/CQDs, CQDs. It was found that the absorbance value of the reaction solution with Fe,S,N/CQDs is significantly higher than that of the reaction solutions with the other four materials (Fig. 3d). This demonstrates that both S and Fe, N elements have a certain promoting effect on the POD-like activity of CQDs. When Fe is introduced into CQDs, its catalytic activity is greatly enhanced (the catalytic activity of Fe,S/CQDs is much higher than that of CQDs, which can be confirmed). Fe,S,N/CQDs exhibit higher catalytic activity than Fe,S/CQDs and Fe,N/CQDs, which might occur because there is a synergistic effect between S and N [43]. This results in a higher catalytic activity of Fe,S,N/CQDs compared to Fe,N/CQDs without S and Fe,S/CQDs without N. Additionally, through the Zeta potentials of the three materials, it can be observed that both Fe,S,N/CQDs and Fe,N/CQDs are negatively charged (Fig. 3e), which facilitates the binding with positively charged TMB. However, Fe,S/CQDs are positively charged, which hinders the binding with TMB. Moreover, the Zeta potential value of Fe,S,N/CQDs is more negative than that of Fe,N/CQDs, making Fe,S,N/CQDs more favorable for TMB coloration than Fe,N/CQDs.

To investigate the catalytic mechanism of Fe,S,N/CQDs, we used ascorbic acid (AA), sodium benzoate, tryptophan (Try), and isopropanol (IPA) as scavengers to remove the generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) during the reaction process (Fig. 3f). Among them, AA and sodium benzoate can eliminate O2 •−, Try can eliminate 1O2, and IPA can eliminate •OH. After they scavenge the corresponding ROS, the amount of generated oxTMB will also decrease. The decrease in absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm can be used to determine the type and quantity of reactive oxygen species generated during the reaction process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of colorimetric sensor and optimization of detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S9a, the feasibility of the colorimetric sensor for UA detection was validated. Significant changes in absorbance at 652 nm were observed when Fe,S,N/CQDs, UA, uricase, and TMB were mixed together, companied by the appearance of blue oxTMB. This is because the reactions between UA and uricase generate H2O2, which can be catalytically decomposed by Fe,S,N/CQDs to produce ROS, and can further oxidize TMB to oxTMB (Fig. S8). The solution changes from colorless to blue can be observed with the naked eyes and it kept getting darker owing to the raise of the amount of oxTMB when the concentration of UA was increased. Obviously, the absorbance value at 652 nm also increased accordingly. In summary, we can construct a colorimetric sensor based on Fe,S,N/CQDs for UA detection using TMB as the chromogenic substrate.

To further optimize the detection conditions of the colorimetric sensor, we conducted optimization of the material concentrations, reaction temperature, incubation time, pH, and TMB concentration in the reaction. The results, as shown in Fig. S9b–f, indicate that we have selected 0.04 mg/mL Fe,S,N/CQDs and 1.8 mM TMB to participate in the reaction of the colorimetric sensor. And we used 0.1 M HAc-NaAc buffer with pH 4 to maintain the pH stability of the sensor. Finally, the absorbance recordings were taken after incubating at 50 °C for 40 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric sensing detection of H2O2 and UA
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the accuracy and stability of the colorimetric sensor, the relative activities of Fe,S,N/CQDs were measured with different storage times and different synthesis batches (Fig. S10). We found that the enzyme-like activity of the material showed no significant change with increasing storage time. Even after 36 days, the relative activity still reached 97 %, thus affirming its long-term reliability and consistency. There were also no significant differences in activity among different batches of Fe,S,N/CQDs. Furthermore, the reproducibility of the same batch of material under the same conditions was confirmed by repeated experiments (Fig. S11), showing that the catalytic activity was essentially the same. The results the suitablilty of the sensor for applications where sustained performance is essential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective testing of the colorimetric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Before conducting actual sample testing, it is necessary to investigate the selectivity of the colorimetric sensor. The selectivity of the sensor was assessed using various potential interferents in serum and urine, such as inorganic ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, NH4 +), urea, dopamine hydrochloride, glycine, glucose, glutathione, ascorbic acid, and homocysteine. It was found that glutathione, ascorbic acid, dopamine hydrochloride, and homocysteine, as typical reducing substances, would have certain effects on the detection. However, the concentrations of these substances in serum and urine are much lower than the concentration of uric acid. Therefore, in this experiment, the concentrations of these four substances are controlled at 5 μM, while the concentrations of other interferents are 300 μM. The concentration of uric acid is 30 μM. As shown in Fig. 4e–f, the interferents have a minimal impact on uric acid detection. Therefore, this colorimetric sensor has the potential to detect uric acid in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of UA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The pratical application of the developed Fe,S,N/CQDs sensor was tested in human serum and urine. The experiment was carried out three times, and the recovery test was performed. By comparing the test results with those obtained from a biochemical analyzer (Table 3 ), it is evident that the colorimetric sensor demonstrates good accuracy in the actual sample detection of UA. In addition, we also conducted spike-and-recovery experiments on serum and urine samples. The recovery rates for serum and urine samples were 96.5–102.5 % and 95.9–104.8 %, respectively, with relative standard deviations (RSD) of less than 3.3 %. These results conclusively demonstrate that the sensor remains unaffected by matrix effects, thereby affirming the practical application of the sensor in real samples.

A series of carbon quantum dots (Fe,N/CQDs, Fe,S/CQDs, S,N/CQDs and Fe,S,N/CQDs) were prepared through thermal decomposition using persimmon frost as a carbon source. Through the comparison and analysis, we found that S, Fe and N elements all contribute to enhancing the POD-like activity of CQDs. In addition, steady-state kinetic experiments also further tested and verified the excellent catalytic performance of Fe,S,N/CQDs. Based on the POD-like activity of Fe,S,N/CQDs, a colorimetric sensor for detecting H2O2 and UA was successfully constructed, and the sensor exhibited a low LOD of 0.73 μM, ensuring its sensitivity to trace levels of UA in real samples. Moreover, its selectivity towards UA enabled accurate measurements in complex samples like biological fluids, making it an effective analytical tool for various real-time applications. These results indicate the tremendous potential of our method in practical applications.

Jiali Zhong: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Yining Zhang: Resources, Investigation. Chunyan Yan: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jin Chen: Methodology. Xiaomin Tang: Visualization, Resources, Investigation. Peng Xu: Validation, Data curation. Ping Qiu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765015, 21808099), China and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001), China.

The study was approved by the ethics committee of Jiangxi Medical College, and their guidelines were followed throughout this study. The serum samples involved in our research were from gout individuals. Informed consent was obtained from all human participants.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110529.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
